item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It turns you into a 6 year old with a lethal weapon that you think is safe. Upvote for creativity

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The fact that this is a very blatant inversion of the effects of SCP-572, and has a very similar memetic effect to SCP-764, may draw the attention of Foundation researchers who would look to see if SCP-1963 is tapping into the same anomaly as the former two anomalies. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Four anomalous properties are associated with SCP-1963 when individuals are in the physical presence of the object. First, an unusually high correlation exists between the use of SCP-1963 and unusual, often improbable, injury to individuals near the object, whether targeted or not; as the object is used more frequently, the seriousness of the injuries and the likelihood of collateral damage to nearby individuals will increase. Second, individuals in the presence of SCP-1963 will consistently and repeatedly express the opinion that the object is harmless and that safety protocols regarding its use are not to be taken seriously; while this behavior is not tantamount to a compulsion to use it, individuals near it will nevertheless invariably attempt to use the object in a humorous manner. Third, individuals encountering injuries inflicted by SCP-1963 will consistently express the opinion that such injuries are less serious or less likely to cause permanent damage than an equivalent injury inflicted with another device, even by the injured individual; this makes SCP-1963 even more dangerous, as injured individuals will not seek medical care unless compelled to do so, and even trained doctors will fail to acknowledge the seriousness of the damage, even after the onset of secondary symptoms or death. The latter two effects present themselves even among individuals previously informed of SCP-1963's abilities. Lastly, individuals that use SCP-1963 will spontaneously acquire objects to launch.

To this:

Four anomalous properties are associated with SCP-1963 when individuals are in the physical presence of the object. First, an unusually high correlation exists between the use of SCP-1963 and unusual, often improbable, injury to individuals near the object, whether targeted or not; as the object is used more frequently, the seriousness of the injuries and the likelihood of collateral damage to nearby individuals will increase. Second, individuals in the presence of SCP-1963 will consistently and repeatedly express the opinion that the object is harmless and that safety protocols regarding its use are not to be taken seriously; while this behavior is not tantamount to a compulsion to use it, individuals near it will nevertheless invariably attempt to use the object in a humorous manner. Third, individuals encountering injuries inflicted by SCP-1963 will consistently express the opinion that such injuries are less serious or less likely to cause permanent damage than an equivalent injury inflicted with another device, even by the injured individual; this makes SCP-1963 even more dangerous, as injured individuals will not seek medical care unless compelled to do so, and even trained doctors will fail to acknowledge the seriousness of the damage, even after the onset of secondary symptoms or death. The latter two effects present themselves even among individuals previously informed of SCP-1963's abilities. Lastly, individuals that use SCP-1963 will spontaneously acquire objects to launch.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between the anomalous properties of SCP-1963, SCP-574, and SCP-764 has been noted. Investigation into whether the effects observed in these three anomalies are variations of a base anomaly is ongoing.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: darth dxVxb, as per the site rules and required reading, spam and contentless posts are against the rules. Please refrain from making such posts in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ITS KETER!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like the idea of level 5 reserchers. I mean it's all very well lvl4 people researching their various bits, but the foundation would probably also want people with eider view who can look for links or patterns between scps and are clever people who know all about what's really going on and can have a think about it (I'd guess O5 are too busy running stuff and firefighting and being enigmatic to have time to sit around theorycrafting). Also it would make sense if lvl 5 researchers were - like O5 supposedly are - forbidden from actual being near scps. That gives a nice firewall for scps that might turn out to corrupt or mess up the regular local researchers (like this one in fact quite a few others)
Sorry for long post just thinking out loud….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Level 5 research personnel?
Is this something people aren't supposed to know about?
There's level D security for disposables, level 0 for a few rare friendly and helpful sapient SCPs, level 1 , level 2, level 3, and level 4 for research and administration, and then level 5 is the O-5 council who run the whole Foundation from a top-secret base somewhere. The council is strictly forbidden to get within kilometers of any SCP because paranoia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DENNIS!!!

Really though, thorough and well written, good logs and I found the mind bending just original enough. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't be the only person to start humming the Benny Hill Show theme-tune whilst reading the final test-log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is pretty much the only time I feel that lolFoundation (as I've come to understand it recently) is really justifiable.

I detect some staying-power in this one.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downs, if I ever catch you baiting another user with this kind of inflammatory behavior again, there will be consequences. You have been warned.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm just going to call for a stop to this conversation right now. That includes responding to any of the other posts in this sub-thread.

The rest of the article is fair game for discussion though.

item.title: Re: long time lurker here
item.content:encoded: Lets not be so quick to say he wasn't a lurker. I myself lurked here for several months before finally joining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a different opinion than you do, and you're getting defensive and angry about it.

Somebody's projecting

What we're trying to say is that judging an SCP based on it being seemingly cliche is a rather poor system of voting. You should stop and take into account whether or not the SCP actually pulls off the cliche. If you still find it can't, then feel free to downvote; but if you're downvoting something because it's vaguely similar a theme you've seen before or have been told to watch out for, you're not really judging the article, only the concept.

It's all good though. Just make sure next time to provide more specific feedback as to why you didn't enjoy the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've actually been reading this site for years now.
Time spent as a member =/= time spent on the site.

I think that instead of mocking me for disliking something you like, you should simply accept the fact sometimes people don't have the exact same opinion as you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a different opinion than you do, and you're getting defensive and angry about it. Phrase it however you want, that's what you're doing and it's not very mature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just the fact that that it was yet another mind-altering SCP seriously ruined it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Paragraph breaks needed in several places for flow purposes, and I'm a little confused by the containment procedures… Why would the site manager and two L-4's be necessary to access it, but only L-5's can use it for research? Are L-5's higher-ranked than the people who run the Site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Watch out guys, he's been a member for 17 days, he knows his shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is either not particularly good criteria for voting on an SCP, or a poorly phrased feedback post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could stand to seriously break up the second paragraph in the Description.

Still, +1'ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hopefully that wasn't the only criterion you judged this on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP. That makes you think it's totally harmless when it's obviously not.

How original. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how in the description this explicitly lists three anomalous properties in great detail, yet completely omits that it apparently has repeatedly generated material to be shot if none is available.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like you could play up the fratboy humor a little more =p

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's actually the year Dennis the Menace went off of TV.

No joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering…the number for this is 1963. So was this a coincidince or does the author have some dark humour involving a late president.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"You'll shoot your eye out, kid!"

+1 for nostalgia

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but it seems a little wordy at the beginning.

…such testing as was successfully carried out appears to show that the wood is composed of common white oak…

That's a lot of words for 'it's made of wood'. I understand if it was meant to imply the difficulty of direct testing, but it just seems a bit roundabout to me.

Upvoted nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see that now, but I never would have guessed it. My train of thought was "Thing that is launched at anything funny—-> Thing that launches and is funny".

Anyway, my only issue with it is the spontaneous creation of mass. What if instead of randomly-generated candy, they used their uniform buttons? And then, when those get taken away, they just get more creative in 'playing' with it, like yanking out their teeth or just snapping each other with the empty sling?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I thought it was inspired by the movie A Christmas Story, where a child wants a BB-gun as a Christmas present, but his mother insists he'll shoot his eye out with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems really similar to SCP-764, but a bit blander.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Katana of Apparent Invincibility, actually. A very dangerous object that its users literally cannot imagine being any sort of a threat.

But I like the idea of inverting the tomatoes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an inversion of the tomatoes, right?

Also, startlingly creepy.
